text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
MRN: [REDACTED]
DATE OF PROCEDURE: [REDACTED]
DATE OF ACCESSION: [REDACTED]
DATE OF FINAL REPORT: [REDACTED]
ORDERING CLINICIAN: Otolaryngology / Head and Neck Surgery
ATTENDING PATHOLOGIST: [REDACTED]
CLINICAL HISTORY: 52-year-old female, never-smoker, presenting with 11-week history of left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia, referred otalgia, and persistent left level II cervical lymphadenopathy. Recent CT neck demonstrated a 2.2 cm enhancing mass at the left base of tongue with left-sided level IIA lymphadenopathy, the largest node measuring 2.7 cm with central necrosis. Biopsy performed for tissue diagnosis.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED:
  Specimen A: Left base of tongue, directed biopsy (flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy with biopsy under topical anesthesia, clinic-based procedure)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A: Received fresh in a labeled container, "left base of tongue biopsy." The container holds multiple soft tissue fragments, pale tan-white to pale pink, ranging in greatest dimension from 0.2 cm to 0.6 cm, with an aggregate greatest dimension of approximately 1.2 cm. The fragments are irregular, somewhat ragged in contour, and show a firm to rubbery consistency. All fragments are entirely submitted for processing in one cassette, labeled A1.

Representative sections submitted per standard protocol. Sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Immunohistochemical studies performed as noted below.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of the left base of tongue biopsy reveal fragments of squamous epithelium with underlying lamina propria and lymphoid stroma. Within the submitted material, there is an infiltrative neoplasm composed of nests, cords, and irregular sheets of polygonal epithelioid cells with moderately enlarged nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate amounts of pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear pleomorphism is moderate. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. A background lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate is present.

The tumor demonstrates a non-keratinizing to minimally keratinizing morphology, with absence of extensive individual cell keratinization or surface keratin pearl formation. There is no prominent intercellular bridge formation or overt surface dysplasia, features consistent with a tonsillar/oropharyngeal origin. The carcinoma shows infiltrative growth with percolation through the lymphoid stroma. There is no definitive perineural invasion or lymphovascular invasion identified within the limited biopsy material, though assessment is inherently limited.

The overlying surface squamous epithelium, where present, appears intact without evidence of surface dysplasia or carcinoma in situ.

Overall, the morphologic features are consistent with a moderately differentiated, non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, histologically compatible with an oropharyngeal / base of tongue primary site.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES:

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed on Specimen A, block A1):

  p16 (INK4a, CINtec clone E6H4, Roche/Ventana): POSITIVE -- Strong, diffuse nuclear AND cytoplasmic staining in >70% of tumor cells, in a block-positive pattern. This result meets the established threshold for surrogate HPV-associated status in oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (defined as >=70% nuclear and cytoplasmic staining of at least 2+ intensity).

  CK5/6 (D5/16B4): Positive; strong membranous and cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells, confirming squamous lineage.

  p40 (BC28): Positive; nuclear staining confirming squamous differentiation.

  Ki-67 (MIB-1): High proliferative index, estimated 60-70% of tumor cell nuclei positive.

IN SITU HYBRIDIZATION:

  HPV RNA ISH, high-risk subtypes (RNAscope HPV-HR probe, includes subtypes 16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58; Advanced Cell Diagnostics): POSITIVE -- Punctate (nuclear dot) pattern of staining identified in tumor cell nuclei, consistent with episomal and/or integrated high-risk HPV RNA expression. Positive and negative controls performed satisfactorily.

Note: HPV genotyping by PCR or subtype-specific ISH is NOT routinely performed on initial biopsy and is not included in this report. Further molecular characterization may be performed at the discretion of the treating team.

Note: EBV-encoded small RNA ISH (EBER) was NOT performed, as the histomorphology and clinical context are not suggestive of an EBV-related neoplasm. If clinically indicated, this can be performed on request.

ADDITIONAL STUDIES PENDING (to be reported as addendum if ordered):
  - PD-L1 immunohistochemistry (22C3 pharmDx or equivalent) -- ORDERED, result pending per oncology request. Will be reported as addendum upon completion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS:

A. LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, DIRECTED BIOPSY:

   SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED, NON-KERATINIZING TYPE.

   -- p16 immunohistochemistry: STRONGLY POSITIVE (block-positive pattern, >70% cells), consistent with HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
   -- High-risk HPV RNA ISH: POSITIVE (punctate nuclear pattern).
   -- Morphology: Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma; keratinizing morphology absent.
   -- See comment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:

The combination of non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma morphology, strong diffuse p16 block-positive immunoreactivity (meeting ASCO/CAP criteria), and positive high-risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization is diagnostic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. This finding is highly concordant with the clinical and radiologic presentation of a left base of tongue primary with ipsilateral cervical lymphadenopathy in a 52-year-old female never-smoker.

Per current CAP/ASCO/AAO-HNS guidelines, the combination of p16 IHC (block-positive) AND confirmatory HPV RNA ISH positivity provides definitive HPV-associated status classification for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No further HPV-specific testing is required for staging or prognostic purposes; however, genotyping (likely HPV-16 in this epidemiologic context) may be pursued at the treating physician's discretion.

Differential diagnostic considerations including nasopharyngeal carcinoma (EBV-associated) and metastatic carcinoma from a non-oropharyngeal primary site are considered less likely given the site of biopsy, morphologic features, and HPV/p16 positivity.

Correlation with clinical, endoscopic, and imaging findings is recommended. Formal staging per AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th edition (HPV-mediated oropharyngeal SCC) is advised.

Addendum report for PD-L1 result to follow.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Electronically signed by:
[Attending Pathologist, MD]
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine
[Institution Name]

Report status: FINAL (Addendum pending for PD-L1)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
